subsystem: read_ujpg() — the header/recovery-metadata reader for the Lepton JPEG recompression container (the '.lep'/UJG format). It parses an attacker-supplied compressed file: it reads a fixed 4-byte size, inflates a zlib- or brotli-compressed header blob into an in-memory reader (header_reader / MemReadWriter), then walks a sequence of 3-byte ASCII markers (HDR, P0D/PAD, CRS, HHX, FRS, GRB, PGR/PGE, SIZ, EEE, CNT, CMP), each followed by a 4-byte little-endian length and a variable-length payload used to reconstruct the exact original JPEG.
provenance: Identified by the Sirikata namespace, Sirikata::JpegAllocator<uint8_t>, ZlibDecoderDecompressionReader::Decompress, Sirikata::BrotliCodec::Decompress, MemReadWriter, IOUtil::ReadFull, LEtoUint32, colldata/g_decoder, and the 'ujpg' marker naming — these are all from Dropbox's Lepton JPEG tool (github.com/dropbox/lepton), where read_ujpg lives in jpgcoder.cc. The in-code banner '/* sample: primevul_train_2202_vuln */' is UNTRUSTED metadata and was ignored for judgement; the file was reviewed blind on its merits.
trust_boundary: The entire input is untrusted: str_in and the inflated header_reader stream carry bytes that come straight from a user-supplied .lep file (line 8 explicitly notes seccomp is enabled 'before reading user data'). Every LEtoUint32(ujpg_mrk) value — compressed_header_size, hdrs, the CRS count, scnc, grbs, prefix_grbs, max_file_size, and the EEE fields — is a raw 32-bit little-endian integer chosen by the attacker. All marker payloads and all length fields are therefore hostile and may take any value in 0..0xFFFFFFFF unless the code explicitly bounds them.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The function returns bool and takes no arguments; it operates on globals/members such as str_in, str_out, header_reader, colldata, and g_decoder. It declares a single fixed 64-byte scratch buffer ujpg_mrk that is reused throughout to hold the 3-byte marker tags and the 4-byte (and once 28-byte) length reads. The comment on line 8 confirms the security posture: seccomp is enabled here because everything read after this point is attacker-controlled user data. write_byte_bill is accounting (billing) telemetry and has no bearing on parsing.

The first real read pulls 4 bytes from str_in and, unlike most later reads, correctly checks the return value (== 4) and aborts on a short read. LEtoUint32 decodes those 4 bytes little-endian into compressed_header_size (uint32_t). This is one of the few length fields that IS bounded: line 19 rejects anything over 128 MiB for both compressed_header_size and max_file_size, capping the immediately following allocation. This is the model the later length fields should have followed but do not.

When no header_reader exists yet, a JpegAllocator-backed vector of exactly compressed_header_size bytes is allocated (bounded to <=128 MiB by the prior check) and filled from str_in. Note the ReadFull return value here is NOT checked, so a short read leaves the tail of compressed_header_buffer as freshly-zeroed vector storage rather than real data. A fresh MemReadWriter is heap-allocated to become header_reader — the in-memory stream that all subsequent marker parsing reads from.

For UJG format version 1, the compressed header is zlib-inflated. A dedicated no_free_allocator is configured with a 32 MiB arena. Decompress takes (src ptr, src size, allocator, max_output_size). The max output is passed as max_file_size + 2048, which bounds the decompression bomb — but note max_file_size is itself attacker-influenced (it can be reset later by the SIZ marker). The result is a pair<vector,JpegError>; a truthy .second (error) aborts. On success the inflated bytes are swapped into header_reader, which becomes the source stream for the marker loop, and zlib_hdrs records the compressed size for billing.

For any other ujgversion the header is brotli-inflated instead, with the max output bound expressed as max_file_size * 2 + 128 MiB. Because max_file_size is a uint32_t and is attacker-influenced, the expression max_file_size * 2 can itself overflow/wrap before the constant is added, weakening the intended cap. Otherwise the flow mirrors the zlib branch: on decode error abort, else SwapIn the inflated bytes into header_reader. Billing then records the compressed size.

If header_reader already existed (concatenation mode), the code merely asserts that compressed_header_size is 0. Then grbs/grbgdata are seeded to the 2-byte EOI marker (FFD9) as the default trailing-garbage value, to be overwritten if a GRB marker is later found. These globals track the 'garbage' bytes that follow the JPEG stream and are needed for byte-exact reconstruction.

The first mandatory marker is 'HDR'. Its 4-byte length is decoded into hdrs (LEtoUint32) with NO upper bound (unlike compressed_header_size). aligned_alloc(hdrs) allocates a raw buffer; then memset(hdrdata, 0, hdrs) zeroes it, and only AFTER that does the code test hdrdata == NULL. Finally hdrs bytes are read into hdrdata. The ordering (memset before NULL check) and the missing size cap are both defects — see 'what_could_go_wrong'. None of the three ReadFull calls here has its return value checked.

If the first marker is not 'HDR' the function errors out. Otherwise it derives memory_optimized_image from the file type and progressive flag, then calls setup_imginfo_jpg to parse the just-read hdrdata into JPEG component/scan metadata. A failure there aborts. The security of setup_imginfo_jpg depends on the contents of hdrdata, which is fully attacker-controlled.

The next mandatory marker carries pad-bit info. Both 'P0D' and 'PAD' read exactly one byte into padbit via reinterpret_cast<unsigned char*>(&padbit). The subsequent comparisons (padbit == -1) reveal padbit is a signed, multi-byte type, so a 1-byte Read leaves the upper bytes of padbit at their prior value. The PAD branch validates the value is in {0,1,-1} and remaps 1 to 0x7f. Any other marker aborts.

The main recovery loop reads 3-byte markers until the stream runs short. The 'CRS' branch decodes a 4-byte count and calls rst_cnt.resize() with that raw attacker value (up to ~4 billion), then reads 4 bytes per element. The resize is unbounded — a large count forces a multi-gigabyte allocation before any per-element read. .at() is bounds-checked so no OOB write, but the allocation itself is the resource hazard.

The 'HHX' branch compares only the first two bytes ('HH'), using ujpg_mrk[1] and ujpg_mrk[2] as an embedded length via get_remaining_data_size_from_two_bytes. to_alloc = that + 2, so it is always >= 2 and to_alloc - 2 is non-negative; a vector of to_alloc bytes is filled (first two from the marker, the rest read from the stream) and deserialized into thread_handoff. Because the size comes from two bytes it is bounded to <= 65535+2, so this path is comparatively safe.

The 'FRS' branch decodes a 4-byte scan count scnc, then grows rst_err by (scnc - rst_err.size()) zero elements, then reads scnc bytes into rst_err.data(). The insert count is computed as an unsigned subtraction where the left operand (scnc, zero-extended to size_t) can be smaller than rst_err.size() — the underflow makes this the sharpest bug in the file (detailed below).

The 'GRB' branch reads a 4-byte size into grbs (unbounded), allocates grbs bytes with aligned_alloc, then memsets — but the length argument is sizeof(grbs) (the 4-or-8-byte size of the variable) rather than grbs, so only the first few bytes are zeroed. It then NULL-checks grbgdata AFTER the memset, and finally reads grbs bytes. Both the wrong memset size and the post-memset NULL check are defects.

The 'PGR'/'PGE' branch handles prefix garbage (bytes before the JPEG). 'PGE' additionally sets embedded_jpeg. It mirrors the GRB pattern exactly: an unbounded 4-byte prefix_grbs, aligned_alloc(prefix_grbs), memset(prefix_grbgdata, 0, sizeof(prefix_grbs)) — again the wrong (variable-size, not buffer-size) length — a post-memset NULL check, then a read of prefix_grbs bytes.

The 'SIZ' branch overwrites the global max_file_size with a fresh attacker 4-byte value — the same variable that was earlier used as the decompression output bound and the <=128 MiB gate. The 'EEE' branch reads 28 bytes into the 64-byte ujpg_mrk (in-bounds) and decodes seven 32-bit fields at offsets 0..24 (max access ends at byte 28, within the buffer) into truncation bounds passed to colldata. The EEE reads are safely bounded by the 64-byte buffer.

The loop terminator handles 'CNT' (set pending_header_reads and break, keeping header_reader alive for continued reads) and 'CMP' (break normally). Any unrecognized marker prints 'unknown data found', sets errorlevel, and returns false. Note the control flow: after the CNT/CMP break the loop exits; the 'return false' only executes for the unknown-marker case because both known cases break out first.

Unless CNT requested pending reads, header_reader is freed and nulled. Billing records 2 + hdrs + prefix_grbs + grbs (a sum of attacker-controlled sizes that can wrap, but only affects telemetry). A final 3-byte marker is read from str_in and must equal 'CMP' or the function aborts. Finally the decoder is armed: signal_worker_should_begin, g_decoder->initialize(str_in, thread_handoff), and start_decoder — handing the now-parsed metadata to the actual image decode.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: scnc is a raw attacker 32-bit value from LEtoUint32; rst_err.size() is a 64-bit size_t. The insert count scnc - rst_err.size() is computed in unsigned size_t arithmetic. If scnc < rst_err.size() — trivially achievable because the FRS marker can appear multiple times in the while loop (the first FRS grows rst_err, a second FRS with a smaller scnc makes the subtraction underflow) — the count wraps to a value near 2^64. std::vector::insert then attempts to add ~1.8e19 elements, throwing std::length_error/std::bad_alloc; since read_ujpg does not catch it, the process terminates (remote DoS on any hostile .lep file). Even when scnc >= size, scnc is unbounded so a single large FRS forces a multi-gigabyte allocation. Invariant: the implementation must reject scnc that is smaller than the current rst_err size and bound scnc against the remaining stream length BEFORE subtracting/inserting.

Two defects. (1) hdrs is an attacker 32-bit length with NO upper bound (contrast compressed_header_size, capped at 128 MiB on line 19); aligned_alloc(hdrs) can be asked for up to ~4 GiB — an uncontrolled resource allocation / easy OOM. (2) memset(hdrdata, 0, hdrs) runs BEFORE the NULL check on line 86, so when aligned_alloc fails and returns NULL, the code writes hdrs zero bytes starting at address 0 — a NULL-pointer dereference (and, with a large hdrs, a wild write far past page 0) that crashes the process. Invariant: bound hdrs against a sane maximum and against the header stream length, and check hdrdata != NULL BEFORE touching it.

grbs is an unbounded attacker length driving aligned_alloc(grbs) (uncontrolled allocation). The memset uses sizeof(grbs) — the size of the length VARIABLE (4 or 8 bytes), not grbs — so the freshly allocated buffer is left almost entirely uninitialized; if the subsequent ReadFull short-reads (its return value is not checked on line 175), grbgdata's tail is consumed uninitialized. Additionally the NULL check comes AFTER the memset, so an allocation failure still dereferences NULL in memset (albeit only sizeof(grbs) bytes). Invariant: bound grbs, check the pointer before use, and memset/read the full grbs length while honoring ReadFull's return value.

Identical defect class to GRB but on a distinct anchor (prefix garbage). prefix_grbs is an unbounded attacker length feeding aligned_alloc; memset(prefix_grbgdata, 0, sizeof(prefix_grbs)) again zeroes only the size of the variable, not the allocation; and the NULL check follows the memset. On allocation failure memset dereferences NULL; on success the buffer is left mostly uninitialized and its tail can leak if the following ReadFull (line 193, return unchecked) short-reads. Invariant: same as GRB — cap the size, NULL-check first, initialize/read the full buffer.

The CRS count is a raw 32-bit attacker value passed straight to rst_cnt.resize() with no bound. A value near 0xFFFFFFFF forces rst_cnt to allocate ~4 * 4e9 bytes (rst_cnt elements are 32-bit) up front, before any of the per-element reads that would fail on a short stream — a memory-exhaustion / bad_alloc DoS from a tiny input. Invariant: cap the CRS count against the remaining stream length (each element needs 4 more bytes) before resizing.

The SIZ marker overwrites the global max_file_size with an unvalidated attacker value. max_file_size is used elsewhere as the decompression output bound (max_file_size + 2048 for zlib, max_file_size * 2 + 128 MiB for brotli on lines 46 and 60) and as the <=128 MiB acceptance gate on line 19. Because SIZ can appear in the same file, an attacker can set max_file_size to a large value (or one that makes max_file_size * 2 wrap) to weaken those bounds for any later or re-entrant decode, and str_out->call_size_callback(max_file_size) on line 11 propagates it. Invariant: validate max_file_size against the 128 MiB policy whenever it is (re)assigned, and never let a wire field silently relax a security bound.

padbit is compared against -1 on line 116, so it is a signed multi-byte integer, yet only one byte is read into it via reinterpret_cast<unsigned char*>(&padbit). On a little-endian host this sets only the low byte and leaves the remaining bytes at their prior (possibly uninitialized) value, so the subsequent {0,1,-1} validation and the padbit==1 remap operate on a partially-defined value. reinterpret_cast also performs no alignment/type check. Invariant: read into a correctly sized 1-byte staging variable and assign, or zero-initialize padbit before the partial read; do not reinterpret a wider object as a byte buffer for a 1-byte read.

Systemic issue: nearly every ReadFull in this function ignores its return value (lines 26, 78, 82, 92, 107, 135's siblings, 139, 142, 151, 156, 161, 165, 175, 183, 193, 197, 201, 233). Only line 13 checks it. On a truncated stream, ReadFull returns fewer bytes than requested and the code proceeds to (a) LEtoUint32 stale ujpg_mrk bytes, driving allocations off garbage, and (b) treat partially-filled buffers as complete. Combined with the wrong-size memsets on lines 168/186, downstream consumers (setup_imginfo_jpg, the decoder) process uninitialized memory. Invariant: every ReadFull must be checked and a short read must abort before the value is used.

summary: This is Lepton's read_ujpg header parser, which decodes a fully attacker-controlled .lep container. The sharpest bug is an unsigned-underflow at line 159 (scnc - rst_err.size()) that turns a small FRS length into a near-2^64 vector insert (uncaught-exception DoS); alongside it are several uncontrolled attacker-sized allocations (hdrs, grbs, prefix_grbs, rst_cnt/scnc) with no upper bound, two memsets that use sizeof(size_var) instead of the buffer length (leaving buffers uninitialized), NULL checks placed after the memset that dereferences the pointer, and a SIZ marker that rewrites the max_file_size security bound. Overall: multiple resource-management (CWE-399 family) and integer/validation defects reachable from a single hostile file, giving reliable remote DoS and NULL/uninitialized-memory hazards.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Fixed 64-byte scratch buffer; largest single read into it is 28 bytes (EEE) and marker reads are 3-4 bytes, so it is never overrun.
Attacker length but explicitly bounded to <=128 MiB on line 19 before the vector allocation.
Size already capped at 128 MiB; ReadFull return unchecked but no OOB — folded into the systemic unchecked-ReadFull finding.
Output bounded by max_file_size+2048; risk is indirect via SIZ rewriting max_file_size, captured under the SIZ finding.
max_file_size*2 can wrap for large attacker max_file_size; noted under the SIZ / max_file_size finding as the mechanism that weakens this bound.
Allocation/free are paired and gated by pending_header_reads (CNT); no double-free or leak visible in the shown flow.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: hdrs = LEtoUint32(ujpg_mrk) + aligned_alloc(hdrs) + memset before NULL check (lines 83-86)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Unbounded allocation and NULL-deref via memset-before-check.
Correct 3-byte tag comparison; acts as a required-marker gate, no weakness.
Partial read into a wider signed variable via reinterpret_cast leaves upper bytes indeterminate.
Unbounded attacker-controlled resize -> memory exhaustion.
Size derived from two bytes (<=65537); to_alloc>=2 so to_alloc-2 is non-negative and the read/deserialize stay in-bounds.
Unsigned underflow of the insert count -> ~2^64 allocation / uncaught exception DoS.
Unbounded allocation, wrong memset size, and NULL check after memset.
Same defect class as GRB on a distinct anchor.
Rewrites a security bound (decompression cap / 128 MiB gate) from wire data with no revalidation.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: EEE: ReadFull 28 bytes; LEtoUint32 at offsets 0..24 (lines 201-208)
reviewed_anchors.reason: 28-byte read and max offset 24 (+4) stay within the 64-byte ujpg_mrk; no OOB.
Static default assignment of the 2-byte EOI marker; no attacker input.
Control-flow only; governs whether header_reader is freed, no memory-safety issue.
Sum of attacker sizes can wrap but only affects billing telemetry, not memory safety.
Reads exactly 4 bytes from a 64-byte buffer; the risk is the unbounded values it produces, tracked under each consumer.
Short reads are ignored, so stale/uninitialized bytes drive allocations and downstream parsing.

findings.title: Unsigned underflow in FRS insert count -> ~2^64 allocation / uncaught-exception DoS
findings.bug_class: Integer underflow driving an unbounded container growth (resource exhaustion / DoS)
findings.invariant: scnc must be validated (>= current rst_err.size() and <= remaining stream bytes) before it is used to compute an insert count; the growth amount must never be a wraparound of scnc - rst_err.size().
findings.failure_mode: An attacker sends an FRS marker whose scnc is smaller than the current rst_err.size() (e.g. a second FRS after a larger first one). scnc, zero-extended to size_t, minus the larger size_t size underflows to ~1.8e19. std::vector::insert attempts that many elements, throwing std::length_error/std::bad_alloc which read_ujpg does not catch, terminating the process. Even without underflow, an unbounded scnc forces a huge allocation. Remote DoS from a single crafted .lep file.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether scnc is bounded against rst_err.size() and the remaining header_reader length before the insert, and whether any caller wraps read_ujpg in a try/catch that would downgrade the crash.

findings.title: Unbounded hdrs allocation with memset before NULL check (NULL-pointer write)
findings.anchor: hdrdata = (unsigned char*) aligned_alloc(hdrs); memset(hdrdata, 0, hdrs); if ( hdrdata == NULL )
findings.bug_class: Uncontrolled allocation size + NULL-pointer dereference (write) before the guard runs
findings.invariant: hdrs must be bounded to a sane maximum (as compressed_header_size is on line 19), and hdrdata must be checked non-NULL BEFORE any memset/read; allocation size must be validated before the allocation is used.
findings.failure_mode: hdrs is an unbounded 4-byte attacker value; aligned_alloc(hdrs) can request ~4 GiB and fail, returning NULL. memset(hdrdata, 0, hdrs) then writes hdrs zero bytes starting at address 0 — a NULL-pointer dereference (a wild zeroing write for large hdrs) that crashes the process before the line-86 NULL check is ever reached. Also an OOM/resource-exhaustion vector when the allocation succeeds huge.
findings.what_to_confirm: That aligned_alloc can return NULL here and that no upper bound on hdrs exists elsewhere; reorder to check NULL before memset and add a size cap.

findings.title: GRB garbage: unbounded aligned_alloc + wrong memset length (sizeof var) + post-memset NULL check
findings.bug_class: Incorrect buffer-size calculation (uninitialized memory) + uncontrolled allocation + NULL deref
findings.invariant: The zeroing length must be grbs (the allocation size), not sizeof(grbs); grbs must be bounded; the pointer must be NULL-checked before memset; and ReadFull's short-read must be handled so the buffer is never partially initialized.
findings.failure_mode: grbs is unbounded (uncontrolled allocation). memset uses sizeof(grbs) (4/8 bytes) instead of grbs, so the buffer is left almost entirely uninitialized; if the subsequent unchecked ReadFull short-reads, downstream code consumes uninitialized heap bytes. Because the NULL check follows the memset, an allocation failure still dereferences NULL (small write) before the guard.
findings.what_to_confirm: That grbgdata is later read for grbs bytes regardless of ReadFull success, and that sizeof(grbs) was intended to be grbs.

findings.title: PGR/PGE prefix garbage: same unbounded alloc + wrong memset length + post-memset NULL check
findings.bug_class: Incorrect buffer-size calculation (uninitialized memory) + uncontrolled allocation + NULL deref
findings.invariant: The zeroing length must be prefix_grbs, not sizeof(prefix_grbs); prefix_grbs must be bounded; NULL-check before memset; handle ReadFull short reads.
findings.failure_mode: Identical to the GRB defect on the distinct prefix-garbage anchor: unbounded prefix_grbs allocation; memset zeroes only sizeof(prefix_grbs) bytes leaving the buffer mostly uninitialized (leakable on short read); NULL check after the dereferencing memset.
findings.what_to_confirm: That the wrong memset size and post-memset NULL check are present exactly as in GRB and share the fix.

findings.title: Unbounded rst_cnt.resize from attacker 32-bit count (memory exhaustion)
findings.bug_class: Uncontrolled resource allocation from unvalidated length (DoS)
findings.invariant: The CRS element count must be bounded against the remaining stream length (each element consumes 4 further bytes) before resize.
findings.failure_mode: A CRS marker with a count near 0xFFFFFFFF makes rst_cnt.resize allocate billions of elements up front (before any per-element read can fail), causing bad_alloc/OOM from a tiny crafted input — a memory-exhaustion DoS.
findings.what_to_confirm: That there is no cap on the CRS count and that resize precedes the per-element ReadFull loop.

findings.title: SIZ marker rewrites the max_file_size security bound from wire data
findings.bug_class: Improper input validation / security bound relaxed by untrusted data
findings.invariant: max_file_size must be revalidated against the 128 MiB policy every time it is assigned, and decompression bounds derived from it (max_file_size + 2048; max_file_size * 2 + 128 MiB) must not be able to wrap.
findings.failure_mode: SIZ overwrites the global max_file_size with an unvalidated attacker value that the code elsewhere uses as the zlib/brotli output cap (lines 46, 60) and the <=128 MiB acceptance gate (line 19). A large value (or one that makes max_file_size * 2 overflow) weakens those caps for subsequent/re-entrant decodes and is echoed to str_out->call_size_callback(max_file_size) (line 11).
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether max_file_size assigned here is re-checked before any later use as a decompression or acceptance bound, and whether max_file_size*2 can overflow uint32_t.

findings.title: Systemic unchecked ReadFull return values -> stale/uninitialized data drives parsing
findings.bug_class: Unchecked return value / improper handling of short reads
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/eval/eval_0028_cwe399.cpp (read_ujpg, nearly all ReadFull calls except line 13)
findings.invariant: Every ReadFull must be compared to its requested length and a short read must abort before the returned bytes (marker tags, LEtoUint32 lengths, payloads) are used.
findings.failure_mode: On a truncated stream ReadFull returns fewer bytes than requested but the code ignores this and proceeds to decode stale ujpg_mrk bytes into lengths (driving allocations off garbage) and to treat partially filled buffers (hdrdata, grbgdata, rst_err) as complete, so downstream consumers such as setup_imginfo_jpg and the decoder process uninitialized memory.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether ReadFull can return short (rather than always throwing) and whether any caller checks length; the two wrong-size memsets make the uninitialized-tail exposure concrete.

findings.title: 1-byte read into wider signed padbit via reinterpret_cast (indeterminate upper bytes)
findings.bug_class: Type confusion via reinterpret_cast / partial write leaving indeterminate bytes
findings.invariant: padbit must be fully defined before the {0,1,-1} check; read into a 1-byte staging variable and assign, or zero-initialize padbit before a partial read; do not reinterpret a wider object as a byte buffer.
findings.failure_mode: padbit is compared to -1 so it is a signed multi-byte type, but only one byte is written into it. The remaining bytes keep their prior/uninitialized value, so the subsequent validation and the padbit==1 remap can act on a partially-defined value and behave inconsistently across runs; reinterpret_cast also skips any alignment/type checking.
findings.what_to_confirm: The declared type/width of padbit and whether it is initialized before line 111; confirm endianness assumption of the 1-byte read.

audit_checklist: Confirm scnc's type and that FRS can recur; add a guard rejecting scnc < rst_err.size() and scnc > remaining bytes before the line-159 insert.
Add an upper bound (e.g. the same 128 MiB gate) to hdrs, grbs, prefix_grbs, the CRS count, and scnc before any aligned_alloc/resize.
Reorder every 'aligned_alloc then memset then NULL-check' so the NULL check precedes the memset (lines 84-86, 167-169, 185-187).
Fix the two memsets to use the allocation length (grbs / prefix_grbs), not sizeof(grbs) / sizeof(prefix_grbs).
Check the return value of every ReadFull and abort on a short read before using the bytes; only line 13 currently does this.
Revalidate max_file_size against policy whenever SIZ rewrites it, and ensure max_file_size*2 (brotli bound) and 2+hdrs+prefix_grbs+grbs (billing) cannot overflow.
Ensure padbit is fully initialized before the 1-byte reinterpret_cast Read and the subsequent {0,1,-1} validation.
Verify aligned_alloc's contract (single-argument wrapper) — whether it can return NULL and what alignment/size validation it performs.
